content.text: Every React workflow runs through a terminal. Vite's dev server, npm package management, TypeScript type-checking, Git operations, and test runners are all invoked from the command line. Understanding the terminal fundamentals covered here will make the rest of the development environment feel natural rather than opaque.

attrs.alt: Terminal window showing the essential commands for React development and keyboard navigation shortcuts

content.text: The shell is the program that interprets your commands. Three shells are common in React development environments:
Default on most Linux distros; macOS switched to zsh in Catalina
Default on macOS; better autocomplete; supports Oh My Zsh plugins
Works for npm; some Unix commands differ (use WSL for full compatibility)
All examples in this document use bash/zsh syntax, which is compatible with macOS Terminal, Linux terminals, and Windows WSL. The npm commands work identically in PowerShell.
The terminal always has a current working directory (CWD). Every relative path is resolved from the CWD.

pwd                    # print working directory — shows where you are
ls                     # list files and directories
ls -la                 # list all files including hidden (dotfiles like .env)
cd my-react-app        # change into a directory
cd ..                  # go up one level to the parent directory
cd ~                   # go to home directory
cd -                   # go back to previous directory
In a React project you will spend most of your time at the project root — the directory that contains
must be run from the project root.
Creating and Removing Files and Directories

mkdir src/components            # create a directory
mkdir -p src/features/auth     # create nested directories (-p: no error if exists)
touch src/components/Button.tsx # create an empty file
rm src/components/Button.tsx   # delete a file
rm -r src/old-feature/         # delete a directory and its contents recursively
mv src/Foo.tsx src/foo.tsx     # rename / move a file
cp .env.example .env           # copy a file
There is no trash bin — deleted files cannot be recovered from the terminal. Always confirm the path before running
to get a confirmation prompt before each deletion.

cat package.json               # print file contents
head -n 20 package.json        # print first 20 lines
tail -n 20 package.json        # print last 20 lines
grep -r "useState" src/        # search for a string recursively in src/
grep -n "useEffect" src/App.tsx # search in one file, show line numbers
Environment variables are key-value pairs that configure program behaviour at runtime. In React development there are two layers:
— set in your terminal session with
and lost when the session closes.
are bundled into the client code and accessible via

# View an environment variable
echo $NODE_ENV

# Set one temporarily (this session only)
export API_URL=http://localhost:3001

# View all environment variables
env | grep VITE

# .env (project root — committed for public config)
VITE_API_URL=https://api.example.com

# .env.local (gitignored — secrets per developer)
VITE_PRIVATE_KEY=dev-key-only
or any file containing secrets. Vite's starter template already includes
Every terminal command exits with a numeric code.
means success; any non-zero value means failure. This is how
works: it only runs the next command if the previous one exited with

# Check the exit code of the previous command
echo $?

# Chain: only install if cd succeeds
cd my-app && npm install

# TypeScript check then build — stops on type errors
npm run lint && npm run build

# Run regardless of first command's exit code (use ;)
npm run clean ; npm run build
React development often requires running multiple processes simultaneously — a dev server and a test watcher, for example. Two approaches:
— open a second tab and run the second command there. VS Code's built-in terminal supports multiple panes (
to run a command in the background. Use

# Run dev server in the background
npm run dev &

# Check background jobs
jobs

# Bring job 1 to foreground
fg %1

# Stop all npm dev processes by name
kill $(lsof -ti:5173)
For projects that need concurrent processes routinely, the
package (added as a devDependency) lets you define a single npm script that runs multiple commands in parallel with labeled output.
Viewing and Editing Files from the CLI

# Open current directory in VS Code
code .

# Open a specific file in VS Code
code src/App.tsx

# Quickly view a file without opening an editor
cat src/App.tsx

# Paginate through a long file
less src/App.tsx   # q to quit, / to search

command requires the VS Code shell integration to be installed. Run it from the VS Code command palette: "Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH". After that, you can open any directory in VS Code from the terminal.
Git is the source control system used by essentially every React project. The core workflow is:

git init                       # initialise a new git repo
git status                     # see which files are changed or staged
git add src/App.tsx            # stage a specific file
git add .                      # stage all changed files
git commit -m "add login form" # commit with a message
git log --oneline              # view commit history (compact)
git diff                       # see unstaged changes
git diff --staged              # see staged (about-to-be-committed) changes

# Branch operations
git branch                     # list branches
git checkout -b feature/login  # create and switch to a new branch
git checkout main              # switch back to main
git merge feature/login        # merge the feature branch into current branch

# Remote operations
git remote add origin <url>    # link to a GitHub remote
git push -u origin main        # push and set upstream
git pull origin main           # fetch and merge latest from remote
When you type a command like
, the shell searches a list of directories called
in order until it finds an executable with that name. Understanding PATH matters for React developers in three situations:
to PATH before running scripts, which is why you can write
— these version managers add the selected Node.js version to PATH. If
is not found, the version manager may not have been initialised in your shell config file (
) ends up in a global bin directory on PATH. Local packages (
and are only available inside the project.

# View your current PATH
echo $PATH

# Find where a command lives
which node
which npm

# Check node version
node --version
sends the output of one command as input to the next. Redirection operators

# Find all TypeScript files in src/
ls src/ | grep ".tsx"

# Save npm build output to a log file
npm run build > build.log 2>&1

# Count lines in a file
wc -l src/App.tsx

# Search build output for errors
npm run build 2>&1 | grep -i error

# Append (don't overwrite) to a log
echo "Build started" >> deploy.log
Shell config files run every time you open a terminal. They are where you set persistent environment variables, aliases, and PATH additions:
for React development includes the nvm or fnm initializer, optional Oh My Zsh, and an alias for

# ~/.zshrc

# fnm (Fast Node Manager) init
eval "$(fnm env --use-on-cd)"

# Aliases
alias gs='git status'
alias gl='git log --oneline'
alias dev='npm run dev'

# VS Code in PATH (macOS)
export PATH="$PATH:/Applications/Visual Studio Code.app/Contents/Resources/app/bin"
npx runs a package directly from the npm registry without installing it globally. This is how the Vite scaffolder is typically invoked:

npx create-react-app my-app    # CRA (legacy)
npm create vite@latest my-app  # Vite (modern — uses npx internally)
npx tsc --noEmit               # TypeScript type-check without tsc being global
npx eslint src/                # lint without global ESLint
, npm downloads the latest version of that package to a temp cache, runs it, and discards it. Subsequent invocations use the cache. For a package installed locally in
, npx runs the local version first.
from the wrong folder gives "npm: command not found" or "Missing script: dev". Always run
Vite defaults to port 5173. If you have a previous dev server still running, the new one will try 5174, 5175, etc. Kill the old process:
After cloning a project or switching branches, run
to install the dependencies listed in
directory is gitignored and is never committed to the repository.
EACCES permission errors on global install.
on macOS can break permissions. Instead, use nvm or fnm to manage Node versions — they install packages into your home directory where you have write access.
Some processes intercept Ctrl+C. Try Ctrl+\ to send SIGQUIT, or close the terminal tab and open a new one.
Useful CLI Tools for React Projects
Print project directory tree — useful for sharing structure
Query and format JSON from the terminal (great for package.json)
Fuzzy file/history search — makes Ctrl+R history search far better
Faster grep with .gitignore awareness — excellent for searching React codebases
Interactive process viewer — see which process is eating CPU (e.g. tsc in watch mode)
Projects pin their required Node.js version in a file at the root so all contributors use the same version. Two common conventions:
— used by nvm. Contains a single version string like
in the project root to switch automatically.
— used by fnm. Same content as
in your shell config, fnm switches versions automatically when you cd into the project.

# Create .nvmrc for the project
echo "20" > .nvmrc

# Let nvm read it and switch
nvm use

# See the currently active Node.js version
node --version
npm --version
Understanding a project's directory layout at a glance is useful when joining a new codebase or debugging an import error.

# Print directory tree (exclude node_modules)
tree -I node_modules

# Or use find (built-in)
find . -not -path '*/node_modules/*' -not -path '*/.git/*' -type f

# Count TypeScript files
find src/ -name '*.tsx' | wc -l

# List all package.json scripts
cat package.json | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(k,':',v) for k,v in d.get('scripts',{}).items()]"
VS Code has an integrated terminal accessible via
(backtick) or the View → Terminal menu. It opens at the project root automatically, which is the correct directory for npm commands.
Open a new terminal pane: click the
icon in the terminal panel. Run the dev server in one and Vitest in another.
Split the terminal horizontally: click the split icon next to
. Two panes appear side by side, each with its own shell.
The integrated terminal shares the same PATH as external terminals (assuming your shell config is loaded). You can run
Aliases let you abbreviate long or frequently typed commands. Add them to your shell config file so they persist across sessions:

# ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc
alias dev='npm run dev'
alias build='npm run build'
alias test='npm run test'
alias lint='npm run lint'
alias gs='git status'
alias ga='git add .'
alias gc='git commit -m'
alias gp='git push'
alias gl='git log --oneline --graph --decorate'
After editing the config file, reload it with
(or open a new terminal tab). Aliases apply only to your machine — they are not part of the project repository.
Debugging Network Requests from the CLI
When your React app makes API calls, you sometimes need to test the same endpoint directly from the terminal to isolate whether a problem is in the React code or the API itself.

# GET request — print response body
curl http://localhost:3001/api/users

# GET with pretty-printed JSON (requires jq)
curl -s http://localhost:3001/api/users | jq .

# POST with JSON body
curl -X POST http://localhost:3001/api/users \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"name": "Alice"}'

# Check which process is listening on a port
lsof -i :3001
npx kill-port 3001
When you see "Cannot find module" errors from TypeScript or the bundler, the CLI can help you understand what Node is actually finding:

# Find the exact path Node resolves a package to
node -e "console.log(require.resolve('react'))"

# Check a package's version
cat node_modules/react/package.json | grep '"version"'

# List installed packages (top level)
npm ls --depth 0

# See why a package was installed (what depends on it)
npm why vite
Writing a Simple Shell Script for Project Tasks
For multi-step tasks that are run repeatedly, a small shell script is more reliable than remembering a chain of commands. Create a file with a

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/fresh-install.sh — delete and reinstall node_modules cleanly
set -e  # exit immediately if any command fails

echo "Removing node_modules..."
rm -rf node_modules

echo "Clearing npm cache..."
npm cache clean --force

echo "Installing fresh dependencies..."
npm install

echo "Done."

# Make the script executable (run once)
chmod +x scripts/fresh-install.sh

# Run it
./scripts/fresh-install.sh
directive is important: without it, the script continues executing even after a command fails, which can leave your project in an inconsistent state.

For intensive terminal use, tmux is a terminal multiplexer that lets you run multiple terminal sessions within a single window and keep sessions alive even after closing SSH connections. While not required for everyday React development, it is common in production and CI environments:

# Install tmux (macOS)
brew install tmux

# Start a new session
tmux new -s react-dev

# Split the pane horizontally (Ctrl+B then %)
# Ctrl+B + %

# Switch between panes (Ctrl+B then arrow key)
# Ctrl+B + ←/→

# Detach from session (keeps it running)
# Ctrl+B + D

# Reattach to session
tmux attach -t react-dev
The terminal is the primary interface for npm scripts, Git, and Node.js tools. All React workflows pass through it.
Run npm commands from the project root (the directory containing
Tab-completion and command history (↑/↓) dramatically speed up daily CLI use.
files are the standard way to pass configuration into Vite's build.
to PATH when running scripts, which is why local tools (vite, tsc, vitest) are usable in npm scripts without global installation.
npx runs a package from the registry without a permanent install — ideal for scaffolding tools and one-off CLI invocations.
